data.title: Why Dividend Stocks Are Essential For What Comes Next
data.description:
Introduction

Today, I’m extremely excited to do two things:

  1. I will discuss a macro development that could be very bullish for dividend stocks. It’s not just a “random” macro discussion but the backbone of my thesis that buying high-quality dividend stocks isn’t just good, but almost essential for many investors.

  2. In light of the first point, I will tell you where I’m looking for income.

So, let’s make this my shortest introduction ever and get right to it!

The Income Contrarians Are Back

In recent months, I have spent a lot of time on “sucker yields.” These are high yields that end up destroying capital, as they come from companies that pay unsustainable dividends. In many cases, these are the investments that don’t result in high income but dividend cuts and capital losses.

Although there are no bulletproof rules on Wall Street, I have often mentioned that I want people to monitor long-term government bond yields. After all, if the 10-year bond pays 4.2%, that’s the kind of income an investor can get without taking a high risk.

If a certain stock pays a much higher yield (a certain premium due to risk differences is always appropriate), it’s either a very unusual opportunity or a trap.

Generally speaking, this means that government bond yields compete with dividend stocks. The lower the yield on “risk-free” bonds, the more attractive a high-quality dividend stock becomes. I’m painting with the broad brush I bring to almost every article, but I think you get the idea.

I even have proof, as the chart below compares the ratio between Realty Income (O) and the S&P 500 (the black line) to the inverted yield on the 10-year government bond. The higher the yield on risk-free bonds, the higher the likelihood that Realty Income underperforms the market. In this case, I’m using Realty Income as a bond-like dividend income proxy.

As the chart above also shows, bond yields are still hovering close to multi-year highs (remember, it’s inverted). Now, this is attracting buyers, according to Bloomberg:

The contrarian approach is gaining relevance after a selloff in Treasuries last week drove 30-year yields toward 5%, the highest since September, before purchasers emerged. For Ed Al-Hussainy, a portfolio manager at Columbia Threadneedle, it makes sense to go against the herd.

That’s in part because of the growing sense that the Federal Reserve will forgo further interest-rate cuts for months with inflation still elevated. The expectation of additional policy easing has been one of the main pillars supporting bets that short-term Treasuries would outperform and drive the yield curve steeper this year. - Bloomberg

Interestingly enough, longer-term bond yields bottomed when the Fed started cutting rates.

This is because the Fed’s cuts do not control longer-term rates. They impact short-term rates. However, the next quote (from the same article) perfectly captures my thesis, which is that I expect both the Fed and the government to use liquidity (i.e., QE and/or fiscal measures) to drive down long-term rates:

Al-Hussainy has another dynamic in mind — the prospect of a government “backstop” should longer-dated yields surge. President Donald Trump, who has made clear his interest in pushing down long-term borrowing costs, issued a directive this month ordering Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to buy $200 billion of mortgage bonds. - Bloomberg

There’s also a clear diversification angle to this thesis:

“Given growth and stock-market performance and corporate profits have been supported by fiscal spending and the AI buildout, if either of those two falter then I think bonds are going to be a much better diversifier to stocks,” he said. - Bloomberg

Moreover, there’s essentially a “fiscal put” here, as the government is running high deficits and an elevated debt ratio. Using the chart below, we see the government has gotten used to running crisis-like deficits during solid economic times. I don’t expect that to change anytime soon, as it would require a lot of cutting to get these numbers back to 2-3%. No party seems to be willing to be the one cutting hundreds of billions in fiscal spending.

This basically means the government is “short” on bonds. That’s just a figure of speech, as it basically means the higher the rates on debt, the harder it becomes to justify this kind of spending. A more professional way would be for me to make the case that the government is essentially short “duration.”

Hence, as we just discussed, with the market pushing yields higher to incorporate inflation and spending risk, the government is responding through soft-QE (the Fed buying longer-term bonds), yield curve control, and Fannie Mae buying mortgage debt.

That’s the core of my thesis and the reason I believe high-quality dividend stocks are extremely important for what may come next.

Why High-Quality Dividend Stocks Are ‘Suddenly’ So Important

For dividend investors like you and me, this creates opportunities. One could even say we’re in for an asymmetrical opportunity. As fancy as that may sound, it’s based on the foundation that 5% could be a “ceiling” in the 10-year bond, the government may have to intervene at that level (the “fiscal put”).

To me, it looks like the biggest risk is to hold cash instead of being invested.

I’m saying that because I believe the macro environment favors central bank and government intervention. Once that happens, we could see higher inflation from higher liquidity and pressure on rates. Essentially, if my thesis is right, it could lead to suppressed real yields and a rush into high-quality stocks.

That’s why I believe I have two options:

  • If I wanted to retire right now, I would feel in a rush to buy high-quality yield before real rates come down.
  • Because I’m 30 and far away from retirement, I need to offset these risks by wisely investing in assets that will grow my net worth at a substantially higher rate than inflation to allow me to rotate into income-producing assets in the future (whenever I may have to retire).

For now, my focus is on the first bullet point.

So, where should we look for exposure?

For starters, most “high-quality” stocks will do well. In this case, I’m talking about companies with sustainable dividend payouts, consistent growth, and business models that provide pricing power. I often use the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) as a proxy for these stocks. While this ETF has underperformed the market by a substantial margin, it’s now making a comeback, as the ratio between the SCHD ETF and the S&P 500 shows.

After roughly three years of underperforming the market, there seems to be capital rotating back into SCHD. Again, I’m using this as a proxy. While I like SCHD, my main point is that its holdings, including the ones below, embody what I consider “high quality” dividend stocks.

Now, on top of that, I’m looking for mispriced opportunities. In other words, I am looking for companies that are trading at valuations implying permanent distress (the market is pricing in really bad news), despite having balance sheets and business models that will survive and thrive for decades.

One of these is ONEOK (OKE), a company I like even more than my investment in Antero Midstream (AM) at current prices. ONEOK recently announced a 3.9% dividend hike to $1.07 per share per quarter. This implies a yield of roughly 5.6%.

As we can see below, the company has never cut its dividend over the past 26 years and just hiked its dividend close to the upper bound of its 3% to 4% annual dividend growth target.

This company, which is a C-Corp that does not issue K-1 forms, has used M&A to become one of North America’s most critical pipeline owners, as the Magellan takeover gave it even more exposure to the Permian Basin and key export centers like the Gulf Coast.

Right now, its stock is down, as the market fears that subdued oil prices can create an environment where output is pressured, lowering demand for pipelines. While that is technically a very valid fear, ONEOK is shielded from weakness, as it has mostly fixed-price income and exposure to fast-growing areas like natural gas liquids.

Although I assume that total oil growth will substantially weaken in the Permian, if not flatline altogether, ONEOK has found ways to address bottlenecks in key areas through M&A and projects like natural gas processing projects in the Delaware Basin (part of the Permian).

Even in 2020 and 2021, it was able to grow its EBITDA.

As OKE trades now at just 8.5x operating cash flow, I see at least 40% gains if it maintains its ten-year multiple of 8.7x. However, as I have written in the past, I see a path to 9-10x operating cash flow if we get a mix of higher oil prices and lower real rates.

In general, I love midstream. To me, this is one of the best places to be for the years ahead. My favorite Master Limited Partnership midstream stocks are MPLX (MPLX) and Western Midstream Partners (WES). I also like their peers, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ET), and C-corps like Kinder Morgan (KMI).

Then there’s Pfizer (PFE). This is, by far, the most controversial idea in this article. Pfizer is in a tough spot, as it has two massive headwinds. The first one is the post-COVID decline, as demand for vaccines has imploded since the pandemic ended. The second problem is the patent cliff, as close to $240 billion in global drug sales is exposed to patent expiration by 2028, according to Parola Analytics. In general, this is a risk in biotech. However, for Pfizer, it is now a perfect storm.

After 2028, it sees exponential growth:

Yes. All our effort in '26, '27, '28, it is to maintain as much the revenues to a reasonable level, so not to go down more than 3%, 4% in '28 and even less in the other years. But there will be a modest decline probably in '26, '27, '28. We went through all the measures that we have taken to improve our margins to at least come to a floor EPS that will help us go through that period. Following '28, '29, '30, '31 and '32, we calculate our projections are for industry-leading exponential growth in the revenues. - Pfizer

By 2028, Wall Street expects EPS to bottom at $2.51, which would imply a 10% earnings yield and a 67% dividend payout ratio, as Pfizer yields 6.7% while I am writing this.

It also has an A-rated balance sheet and a strong M&A focus on fast-growing areas like oncology.

I know that waiting for a turnaround can be annoying, but I think the PFE ticker will trade “much higher” five years from now, as we are dealing with a company trading at 8x earnings, slowly bottoming earnings, and the potential to start a multi-decade growth streak after one of the toughest patent loss cliffs I have ever seen.

And just like OKE, I also like PFE’s peers, as I consider healthcare to be a terrific value-focused sector. Especially with the rise of AI, I think we’ll see innovation go into overdrive. That’s why I even like players like Eli Lilly (LLY) that have gone through the roof already.

My next play is Conagra Brands (CAG), which is a consumer company most of my American readers may be familiar with, as it produces a lot of frozen (and non-frozen) foods that one can buy at Walmart, Publix, and other stores.

The entire industry has gone through a really tough post-pandemic phase, as inflation started to bite margins, consumers started to cut back on some expenses, and sentiment among investors soured tremendously. There was also a political layer, like the pause in SNAP payments, which is visible in the chart below. Overall, volumes have been under pressure for years.

Here’s an overview of the company’s latest results, which show weak volumes and eroded pricing power:

For its 2026 fiscal year, it sees organic sales growth of negative 1% to 1% positive growth.

None of this is exciting. That’s why its stock price looks like this:

But that’s the opportunity. After all, this is the part of the article where I focus on beaten-down value.

As we can see above, the stock is attempting to bottom, which makes sense, as I think we’re past “peak negativity.” The fiscal year of 2026 seems to be the last big flush before analysts expect a recovery to 5% and 7% EPS growth in FY2027 and FY2028, respectively.

Currently, the company trades at just 9.3x FY2028 EPS, which is at least 30% below its long-term multiple of 13.1x. Moreover, it yields 7.8% with an EPS yield of 10.6%.

Once the company proves the decline is a thing of the past, I think this stock will re-rate much higher. I don’t own it because I don’t focus on income, but I think it makes for a great risk/reward income stock, which is obviously the reason it’s part of this article.

Takeaway

I believe we’re on the verge of a major macro shift.

I expect we’ll see a fiscal put where both the Fed and the government will actively suppress long-term rates, likely creating higher inflation risks along the way. So far, we’ve seen evidence in areas like mortgage rates, a more dovish Fed, and a much bigger focus on nominal growth.

That’s why I believe we’ll see the return of high-quality dividend stocks in a world where real rates are poised to be under pressure.

It’s why I am rushing into the highest-quality players I can find, which includes lower-yielding dividend growers/cyclical value with inflation protection, and, for income investors, undervalued dividend income.

In fact, despite the market’s lofty valuation, I have invested every penny of excess cash in recent years, as I think this is a great market to buy exposure instead of sticking with cash.

Risks To My Thesis

A scenario of runaway inflation would “destroy” the fiscal put. It would force a full focus on inflation protection, likely resulting in a 2022-like scenario where liquidity is drained, and markets suffer.

On top of that, for the individual stocks I mentioned, OKE is prone to weak energy sentiment triggered by lower prices. Pfizer is prone to M&A and innovation risk when it comes to offsetting patent losses. CAG’s biggest risk is a further weakening consumer and elevated inflation that offsets pricing.

data.title: General American Investors: We Are Forced To Put A Sell Rating On One Of Our Favorite Preferred Stocks
data.description:
Recently, we have been both investing in and writing articles about CEF preferred stocks. We are turning our attention to credit risk and are willing to bet that long-term yields may finally move lower amid global uncertainty. It is for the world to see whether the USA will still be a safe haven, and the null hypothesis is "yes" unless proven otherwise. Today, we will focus on a very simple yet important lesson about preferred stocks. There are two main points:

  1. Past performance is useless when evaluating preferred stocks
  2. Buying below par is always better when the current yield is the same.

We will have to place a sell rating on one of our favorite preferred stocks through the years, and it comes from a CEF that has been around for almost a century.

General American Investors Co. Inc. (GAM) is a closed-end fund established on February 1, 1927 (yes, we double-checked the date). It has approximately $1.8 billion in total net assets as of 09/30/2025. The fund's leverage is 10.5%, and its expense ratio is 1.11%. The asset coverage ratio at the end of Q3 is 952%, as you can see in the picture below:

GAM has close to 67 holdings, and the biggest part of its portfolio is close to 6% in the Microsoft Corporation (MSFT):

To put it simply, GAM is invested in some of the largest companies in the world whose liquidity is very similar to cash equivalents today. This makes its preferred stock extremely safe from a credit perspective.

In the following lines, we will outline the pros and cons of GAB's preferred stock (GAM.PR.B).

GAM.PR.B

  • Close to 6% Current Yield and High Credit Rating

Below is shown GAM's preferred stock (GAM.PR.B).

GAM.PR.B was issued on 09/24/2003 with a coupon rate of 5.95%. The spread to the treasury yield then was close to 1.25%, while currently it is around 1.13%. Moody's rates the stock with an "A1" credit rating. It trades slightly above par, and its current yield is close to 5.93%. The stock's call date was on 09/24/2008, so it is callable anytime.

You can see where GAM-B stands at the time of writing among its peers - it looks just like them at first glance:

  • Qualified dividends are similar to other preferred stocks from the Gabelli Family:

Below, you can see the tax treatment of dividends:

  • The 1940 Securities Act is the same as with any CEF preferred stock:

Here is a part of GAM-B's prospectus, where you can see that it is under the regulation of the 1940 Act:

The 1940 Act requires the fund to maintain sufficient assets to cover debt plus preferred shares by at least 200%. GAM currently has an asset coverage ratio of 952%, as we mentioned above, and the limit was enforced by regulators to make CEFs safe. The preferred stock is senior to the common stock and is way less leveraged than the regulatory limit.

  • The biggest drawback

At first glance, GAM-B looks like a "buy", and is very similar to other issues that we have rated like that in recent weeks - it has a yield of around 6%, it has a high credit rating, the company has been stable for a long time, the dividends are qualified, and it is protected by a regulatory leverage limit.

If we look at the chart of GAM-B vс GAB-K, we see that GAM-B has outperformed and has been among the safest preferred stocks, even with long-term yields going from 1 to 5%:

At the time, both securities had different downside durations. GAM-B was a pinned-to-par preferred stock, while GAB-K was a low nominal yielder. Currently, the downside duration of the 2 securities is similar, while GAM-B has no upside potential. This can be seen on the chart below:

As the 2 preferred stocks have very similar current yields, their prices will be similarly impacted if, for example, their yields have to shift to 7%. On the upside, though, GAB-K is the one that will outperform significantly.

Conclusion

We are big fans of CEF preferred stocks, and even though we like all of them, we just have to always be invested in the best possible ones from all angles. GAM.PR.B is a prime example of why pinned-to-par preferred stocks offer downside protection against interest rate risk. At the time of writing, though, it is just a worse choice than similar preferred stocks that trade well below par. Even GAM.PR.B is not immune to convexity risk, and this is the only reason we are not buyers.

data.title: 10-12% Yields Trading For Pennies On The Dollar
data.description:
Private credit is being widely painted as a bubble that is about to burst. Headlines have emphasized isolated defaults and painted it as though they are cockroaches that are evidence of many more defaults to come, with JPMorgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon even insinuating as much. Additionally, questions have been raised about liquidity being made available to various funds and about the transparency of marking the loans in private credit funds. As a result, many BDCs (BIZD), including highly reputable ones like Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) and Blue Owl Technology Income Corp. (OTF), have been priced down to steep discounts to net asset value in spite of their strong underlying performance. Additionally, alternative asset managers that have significant exposure to private credit, including the likes of Blackstone (BX), Ares Management (ARES), Apollo Global Management (APO), and Blue Owl Capital (OWL), have all been put in the penalty box as well.

However, my view is that the private credit bear case misunderstands the strong fundamentals and prudent lending practices being implemented by leading direct lending platforms. Moreover, as the data shows, the private credit sector, and in particular BDCs like OTF and OBDC, trade at deep discounts to NAV, which makes them exceptionally attractive buys right now.

Defaults, Loss Rates, And What Actually Matters

As already stated, there are widespread concerns that a few defaults are indicative of many other problems in the private credit sector that will soon come to light, as well as the fact that mark-to-market practices are inflating the performance of the funds. However, these concerns are largely misplaced, especially in the case of higher-quality lenders.

For starters, lenders always have defaults. That is an inevitable part of the investing process. However, what ultimately matters is how large these defaults are, and especially how large the ultimate losses are relative to the overall portfolio. Thus far, private credit has significantly outperformed leveraged loans and high yield bonds over the past 10 and 20 years by a substantial margin, demonstrating that their higher yields more than offset the downside risks over the long-term, including through the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 outbreak.

Moreover, recoveries and diversification are very important when it comes to preserving capital over the long-term. In the case of OBDC and OTF, they both invest primarily in senior secured loans in a first-lien position, and at low loan-to-values, with broadly diversified portfolios, meaning that their average position size is very small. As a result, it is not surprising that they achieve high recovery rates.

In fact, Blue Owl’s direct lending platform, which includes its diversified OBDC and technology-focused OTF, has over $150 billion in private credit assets under management, including $100 billion in direct lending. Moreover, industry loss rates are already quite low, with senior-secured direct lending loans experiencing an annualized loss rate of just 0.29% annually alongside an annualized total return of 8.5% since 2010, which makes senior secured private credit a compelling risk-adjusted investment class compared to high-yield bonds (HYG) and leveraged loans (BKLN), where loss rates tend to be higher (at ~1.47% and ~0.94% annualized, respectively), and returns are often lower (6.5% and 5.8% annualized, respectively).

Why Blue Owl’s Underwriting Stands Out

Blue Owl takes it to the next level with a historic loss rate of just 13 basis points despite its Owl Rock private credit business being in operation for about a decade. Meanwhile, at the specific fund level, OBDC only has 1.3% of its portfolio, on a fair value basis, on non-accrual right now, which is one of the lowest levels in the BDC sector. OTF has the lowest non-accrual rate that I am aware of in the entire publicly traded BDC sector, with just three basis points of non-accruals at fair value, with only two non-accruals in its entire operating history dating back to 2018.

Additionally, most of OTF’s and OBDC’s loans are to private equity-backed companies, in which equity sponsors often own 60%+ of the capital structure beneath the loan. This means there are large, well-funded investors with significant skin in the game who, in many cases, inject additional capital or provide incremental support to struggling companies to help protect their equity investment. This, in turn, provides additional protection for senior secured lenders like OBDC and OTF, helping to keep loss rates low.

OTF takes things to an extra level of safety with a 33% weighted average loan-to-value on its investments and over a 2.0x interest coverage ratio. Meanwhile, OBDC is nearly as conservative, with a 42% average loan-to-value and an interest coverage ratio of 1.9x. As a result, even when a borrower experiences bankruptcy, they often recover $0.70 to $0.90 on the dollar, which helps keep overall loss rates extremely low.

Debunking The Mark-To-Market Transparency Fears

Additionally, concerns about mark-to-market transparency are ill-founded. First of all, these are loans, not equity investments, and therefore they must mature at par, with contractual interest payments that must be made. This makes it far more difficult for direct lenders to manipulate numbers or misrepresent the true health of their portfolios, because these loans are consistently being repaid and maturing, meaning any assessment is ultimately proven right or wrong.

Meanwhile, these companies’ impressive long-term histories of low loss rates verify that they are not obscuring data through their marks and rating systems. Not only that, but every quarter, each portfolio company is valued by a third party, which increases transparency and prevents managers from manipulating valuations themselves.

The OBDC Merger That Wasn’t And Why It Matters

Finally, concerns raised about OWL canceling the merger of OBDC with one of its private funds misunderstand the situation. This was a preplanned structural decision to merge the private fund with the public fund, as both had significant overlap in their underlying investments. At the time, OBDC had a higher dividend yield than the private fund and would have provided investors with immediate liquidity.

However, when the merger was officially announced, OBDC was trading near NAV. By the time the merger was approaching execution, private credit had fallen out of favor, and BDCs like OBDC were trading at steep discounts to NAV. This led some to complain that OWL was disadvantaging private fund investors by converting into publicly traded shares at a time when OBDC traded at a large discount. As a result, management decided to cancel the merger, continue offering quarterly liquidity events in the private fund, and pursue a broader liquidity event at a more appropriate time in the future.

Why OTF Looks Like A Mispriced Technology Credit Play

With these misunderstandings clarified, I believe OTF and OBDC are particularly attractive right now. OTF invests primarily in enterprise software companies with recurring revenue, mission-critical products, non-cyclical demand, and high barriers to entry. These attributes make them far harder to disrupt materially, including from artificial intelligence, or alternatively ensure that even if AI impairs value, the very low loan-to-value ratios keep loans well covered.

The track record is phenomenal, with 18% NAV per share growth since inception on a predominantly lending portfolio, with 80% invested in senior secured loans diversified across 185 companies in 38 end markets. Additionally, the company trades at an over 23% discount to NAV and offers a dividend yield of around 10.6% that should be sustainable over time.

OBDC’s Deep Discount And Durable Income Profile

Meanwhile, OBDC is also very attractive, with 238 portfolio companies providing an extremely diversified investment profile. Approximately 80% of the portfolio is invested in senior secured loans, 91% of investments are sponsor-backed, and cumulative net losses total just 27 basis points, underscoring strong underwriting discipline. Today, OBDC trades at roughly a 19% discount to NAV while offering a dividend yield of 12.3%. This combination of strong underwriting, deeply discounted valuation, and high current income is compelling.

Investor Takeaway

While private credit is always susceptible to an economic downturn that would likely cause non-accruals to rise somewhat, and Federal Reserve rate cuts would likely compress net investment income per share, the deep value, strong underwriting performance, impressive track records, conservative portfolio positioning, and very high yields provide a substantial margin of safety. In my view, this sets up the potential for very attractive risk-adjusted returns over time, barring a catastrophic spike in non-accruals.

As a result, I believe the market is overly bearish on opportunities like OBDC and OTF, as well as several other high-quality BDCs trading at deep discounts to NAV. While I was significantly underweight in the BDC sector last summer due to valuation concerns, today I am prudently and cautiously increasing exposure to take advantage of these discounted prices. This value-investing-driven, opportunistic capital recycling approach is how I seek to drive long-term total return outperformance with low beta in my 7%–8% yielding portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Taiwan's GDP Continues To Defy All Expectations
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

Taiwan's GDP growth continues to defy all expectations

The tech boom amid the AI race continued to pick up in 2025. Taiwan, as the manufacturer of the world's most cutting-edge chips, was one of the prime beneficiaries of this theme, and the 2025 GDP data confirmed this once more.

Taiwan's fourth quarter GDP for 2025 surged to 12.68% year-on-year, the highest YoY read for any quarter since the third quarter of 1987. 2025 GDP ended the year at 8.63% YoY, which was the highest level since 2010. Unlike in 2010, when Taiwan benefited from a very supportive base effect from weak -1.6% YoY growth in 2009 in the wake of the Global Financial Crisis, 2025 saw no such benefits, instead coming on the heels of a strong 2024.

Once again, GDP continues to surprise on the upside, despite a wave of very aggressive upward revisions, as Taiwan's exports easily shrugged off the impact of a US reciprocal tariff hike. Recall that this time last year, markets were looking for growth of under 3%. It turns out that even after a wave of upgrades to over 7%, forecasts continued to undershoot reality.

This strength was almost entirely concentrated on the external side. Net demand from the rest of the world contributed a whopping 11.91ppt of the 12.68ppt of growth in 4Q25, and 6.63ppt of the 8.63ppt of growth for the full year. Domestic demand, as a result, only contributed 0.77ppt in 4Q25 and 2.0ppt for the year as a whole – much less impressive.

This is consistent with what we've been seeing in the monthly data as well. Exports and industrial production have been seeing double-digit growth throughout the year, while retail sales and consumer confidence data have been considerably softer.

4Q25 growth sees the single the highest YoY growth of any quarter since 1987

As the tech boom continues, likely so will Taiwan's run of outperformance

2025 delivered a simple message for Taiwan forecasters across the entire market: we were clearly not bullish enough on the tech boom and its ability to lift Taiwan's growth through net exports.

Is the market about to repeat the same mistake in 2026? Consensus forecasts currently are looking for 3.9% YoY growth in 2026. We are upgrading our 2026 Taiwan GDP forecasts to 6.8% YoY on the back of the strong 4Q outperformance and expectations that the tech-fuelled strength will at least continue for some months ahead, which Taiwan's surging export orders data suggests is likely to be the case for some time.

The biggest single risk to the scenario is the fact that growth remains very highly contingent on the AI boom and tech race continuing. The GDP breakdown clearly illustrates this, as does the fact that TSMC's (TSM) share of the Taiex index has surged to over 44%, up from around 24% in 2020. If we do see an end to this boom, the ripple effects would likely be considerable. In contrast, if we instead see substantive breakthroughs that could guarantee years of strong demand moving forward, Taiwan's longer-term outlook could remain quite bright.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Mainstream Expectations: Hope Vs. Potential Risk
data.description:
Mainstream expectations, those from Wall Street, economists, and corporate strategists, have congealed around a bullish economic outlook for 2026. Most forecasts project stronger economic growth, with contained inflation, and continued investment in technology and capital expenditure. As such, many institutional investors interpret this as a year of opportunity for markets and corporate earnings. That was a point we discussed at this year’s Investment Summit with the following slide.

But it isn’t just earnings that are expected to rise, but due to productivity increases (AI = Less Employment), corporate profit margins are expected to swell to historic records.

However, whenever I see Wall Street becoming universally bullish, the contrarian investor in me is always reminded of Bob Farrell’s Rule #9:

“When all experts agree, something else will happen.”

As I noted in that linked article:

“Excesses are built by everyone on the same side of the trade. Ultimately, when the shift in sentiment occurs – the reversion is exacerbated by the stampede going in the opposite direction.”

Yet the broader risk landscape is significant as consensus optimism obscures important vulnerabilities. When investors anchor on expected outcomes and overlook low‑probability but high‑impact risks, those risks become amplified. History shows that markets rarely transition smoothly from one year to the next without shocks to inflation, monetary policy, geopolitics, or credit conditions. For example, on January 1st, no one expected President Trump to slap additional tariffs on Europe over the potential purchase of Greenland.

But it happened.

So with that, let’s review mainstream expectations for 2026 and detail the “low probability, high impact risks,” that could derail the complacent expectations of investors.

US Economic Growth: Resilience or Fragile Expansion?

Mainstream Expectation: Most economists expect the US economy to grow above trend in 2026. Goldman Sachs forecasts U.S. GDP expanding about 2.6% year‑over‑year in 2026 compared to consensus estimates of roughly 2.0%. Their team sees above‑consensus growth and a strong rebound from 2025.

Other analysts and institutions, including PwC and RSM US, forecast similar growth in the 2.1% – 2.5% range, driven by consumer spending, corporate investment, and broader economic resilience.

Risk to That View: Growth forecasts assume stability in consumer demand, labor markets, and capital spending. But several risks could undermine this:

  • Labor market fragility: Employment growth has slowed sharply in late 2025, and with a declining working‑age population due to lower immigration, net job creation may stay weak. Early data shows average monthly employment growth collapsing to levels historically consistent with labor market stress.
  • Tariff and trade uncertainty: The recent threat of higher tariffs on Europe and continued trade tensions that emerged in 2025 introduce volatility in production and pricing in supply chains. Increased tariffs across major trading partners historically correlate with lower output.
  • Global headwinds: The World Bank warns that while global growth remains resilient, fading dynamism and policy uncertainty could reduce demand for U.S. exports.

Our view is that the most critical risk in 2026 is further weakness in the labor market or trade disruptions worsening, which could cause growth to fall short of expectations.

Inflation and Monetary Policy: Tame or Sticky?

Mainstream Expectation: Consensus forecasts generally expect inflation to moderate through 2026, with core measures heading toward the Federal Reserve’s 2% target. Goldman Sachs projects core inflation close to 2.1% by the end of 2026.

Some money managers expect the Fed to cut rates one or two times in 2026, assuming inflation continues its downward trend and consumer spending remains resilient.

Risk to That View: Given that inflation is a function of economic supply and demand, a “run it hot economy” could keep inflation “sticky” or slightly higher.

  • Sticky core inflation: Some forecasts warn that core inflation may stay above target due to tariff pass‑through, wage pressures, or service inflation. Vanguard’s model suggests core inflation could remain above 2.5% if tariffs and labor tightness persist.
  • Monetary policy divergence: J.P. Morgan’s economist predicts the Fed may actually hold rates steady or even raise them in 2027, due to sticky inflation and labor market strength despite market expectations for cuts.
  • Fed independence risks: Intensified concerns over central bank autonomy could cause further disruptions and uncertainty over future monetary policy direction.

If inflation proves more persistent than expected or if policy credibility erodes, interest rates may stay elevated, weighing on valuations and economic activity.

AI and Corporate Investment: Growth Catalyst or Market Excess?

Mainstream Expectation: Most forecasts see continued strong investment in artificial intelligence and related infrastructure as a driver of both corporate capex and productivity. Most analysts highlight AI’s role in lifting corporate spending and supporting economic expansion.

U.S. corporate bond issuance is also projected to surge, much of it to fund AI data centers, advanced computing infrastructure, and next‑generation platforms.

Risk to That View: The growth from AI investment is uneven and concentrated:

  • Concentration of benefits: A relatively small group of mega‑cap firms capture most of the AI investment gains, which can create sector concentration risk in markets and overstate the breadth of economic benefit.
  • Corporate debt buildup: Higher bond issuance tied to capex, especially for large tech projects, increases leverage risk, especially if growth slows or credit markets retrench.
  • Market pricing risk: A strong investment narrative can inflate asset prices beyond fundamentals, meaning corrections may be abrupt if earnings disappoint.

AI spending is real, but it is not a universal engine for all sectors. Most critically, the overreliance on it for aggregate growth forecasts underestimates broader economic weak spots.

Consumer Spending: Supported or Overstated?

Mainstream Expectation: Analysts expect consumer resilience to remain a backbone of 2026 growth. Strong household balance sheets, robust savings for certain income groups, and wage gains support consumption forecasts. These assumptions pervade GDP models showing above‑trend expansion.

Risk to That View: Consumer dynamics can shift suddenly:

  • Wealth inequality in consumption: Wealth effects are most pronounced among higher‑income households. Median consumers without significant asset holdings may reduce spending if jobs or real income weaken.
  • Debt and credit stress: Higher interest rates increase borrowing costs for households which could depress discretionary spending.

Consumer spending may remain resilient on average, but broad‑based weakness could emerge quietly before appearing in headline data.

The Dollar and Foreign Exchange: Weakening or Volatile?

Mainstream Expectation: Many strategists anticipate a modest depreciation of the U.S. dollar in 2026. As such, a weaker dollar would boost export competitiveness and corporate earnings abroad.

Risk to That View: Currency markets are driven by relative risk and capital flows, not just growth differentials:

  • Growth risk: Stronger economic growth will attract foreign inflows into dollar-denominated assets for higher yields and relative safety.
  • Safe‑haven demand: In times of geopolitical tension or financial stress, the dollar strengthens due to its liquidity and safety. Such would potentially hurt U.S. export competitiveness.

A dollar that strengthens through risk aversion or economic growth would undercut the export growth assumptions embedded in current forecasts.

Tax Policy and Fiscal Stimulus: The Reflation Narrative

Mainstream Expectation: New tax measures, including expanded investment credits and incentives, are expected to boost consumer incomes and corporate spending in 2026. Forecasts incorporate these fiscal tailwinds into growth and profitability models.

Risk to That View: Tax benefits often provide short-lived effects:

  • Timing and bias: Households may smooth additional tax savings into future consumption rather than immediately spend them. Corporations might repatriate savings or use them for share repurchases rather than investing.
  • Dependence Risk: The outlook for increased capex, spending, and earnings are all dependent on economic growth strengthening into 2026. However, as discussed, there are many risks to that view.

Tax incentives are supportive, but they should be viewed as marginal boosts rather than transformational drivers of long‑term growth.

Portfolio Tactics for Investors in 2026

The purpose of this article is not to suggest that Wall Street analysts and market participants are wrong. The purpose is to suggest there are risks to investor portfolios when “everyone is bullish on everything all at once.”

Therefore, given the range of possible outcomes, investors should employ adaptive, risk-aware strategies. Rather than assuming a base-case forecast will materialize, use portfolio tactics to help navigate uncertainty:

  • Diversification Beyond Tech and Growth: Hold a mix of sectors including value, energy, and financials to reduce concentration risk. Consider allocations to fixed income to offset volatility risks.
  • Inflation and Rate Risk Hedging: Maintain allocations to short‑duration bonds to reduce sensitivity to potential rate volatility.
  • Dollar and Currency Exposure Management: Hedge currency risk for international holdings. A stronger dollar could undermine international growth outlooks.
  • Energy and Commodity Positions: Commodities are subject to economic growth. If growth slows, commodities become a higher risk asset.
  • Quality and Balance Sheet Strength: Tilt toward companies with strong balance sheets and stable free cash flow to weather cyclical shocks. Favor dividends and cash returns in uncertain environments.
  • Liquidity Reserves: Maintain higher levels of cash or cash equivalents to capitalize on market dislocations. Liquid reserves provide flexibility should growth disappoint.
  • Tactical Hedging Strategies: Use options or inverse instruments selectively to protect portfolios against sharp downturns. Volatility may rise unpredictably; structured hedges can provide protection without full market timing.
  • Monitoring Macro Signals Actively: Track inflation metrics, labor market data, and Fed communications closely. Be ready to adjust strategies in response to shifts in inflation, policy, or geopolitical developments.

The mainstream outlook for 2026 is cautiously optimistic, grounded in forecasts of steady growth, stable inflation, and continued technology-led investment. Those expectations are reasonable as base cases. However, investors should not mistake forecasts for outcomes. Each major economic assumption carries material risks. Persistent inflation, monetary policy uncertainty, geopolitical shocks, and uneven growth dynamics could all lead to outcomes well outside consensus expectations.

Prudent investors will build portfolios that protect capital first, anticipate volatility, and adapt rapidly to changing economic realities. The probability distribution of 2026 outcomes is wide, and mistakes can be costly when “all the experts agree.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cameco: Switching From Buy-And-Hold, To Buy The Dips And Sell The Rallies (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis.

Cameco (CCJ) was my top pick of the decade back in 2020, and thus far it has not disappointed, with a roughly 1,300% increase in its share price since then. Even though market fundamentals as well as the company's performance make for a strong basis for the dramatic increase in its share price, the rally is arguably overdone, relative to those fundamentals.

I decided to sell my position, despite a bright outlook for the company's financial outlook, because at this point, even the most positive assumptions that one can reasonably make, based on what we know, are more than baked into the price. I intend to start building a position again, by buying incrementally on the way down, in the event of a sizable selloff in its share price.

Going from a buy to a hold.

I last covered Cameco in the spring of last year. I had it as a buy then, on the fundamentals that brought it to current share price levels. At that time, it was trading at a forward P/E ratio of 44 and a share price of $41, as opposed to a share price of $127, as I write this, and a forward P/E ratio of about 124.

As I stated in that article, the buy-and-hold strategy for Cameco stock will come to an end, as it keeps reaching new all-time highs. At one point, we have to consider switching to buying the dips and selling the rallies, as it continues on its way up.

Cameco is a long way from growing into its valuation within a reasonable timeframe.

With a P/E ratio that is roughly 4x higher than the S&P 500 average of about 31, current revenue & earnings growth trends, as well as prospects for the future, may not be enough to justify its current share price. Other metrics do not seem to fit its valuation either.

The first thing I want to look at is Cameco's total uranium reserves.

The total resource base of its uranium mining segment is just over a billion pounds of uranium. The current spot price for uranium is $91/pound, meaning that the total value of Cameco's uranium resources is about $91 billion. Its current market cap is about $55 billion.

Based on estimates for 2025, the cost of its uranium sales per pound was $63 on the higher end. Capital expenditures are expected to be about $9.5/pound. So, the NAV of its current reserves is about $18.5 billion, which is about 1/3 of its current market cap. A threshold Price/NAV ratio of about 1 tends to be seen as a good indicator of whether a stock is a buy or not from this point of view. In other words, a NAV higher than the market cap is preferable from an investor's point of view.

Cameco is more than just a mining company; it has its Westinghouse unit and its fuel services unit. For this reason, it can be argued that simply looking at the net asset value of its uranium reserves is not an adequate measure. The longer-term price of uranium may be much higher in the future compared with the current spot price; therefore, the assumption of higher uranium prices alone is arguably enough to make this company a buy, based on certain assumptions. The fact that Cameco's market cap is three times higher than its total uranium resources thought to be potentially recoverable is nevertheless concerning in my view, which is why I wanted to highlight this specific aspect of its valuation.

Another way to look at whether Cameco is still a good potential investment opportunity at current elevated levels is to determine whether it can grow into its valuation and how long it would take. It has a P/E ratio that is four times higher than the S&P 500. The S&P 500 is itself overvalued by a factor of two by long-term historical standards. If one wants to be conservative, we should assume that Cameco should converge toward a P/E ratio of about 16 in the longer-term, which is in line with long-term market averages.

In the third quarter of 2025, there was a massive leap in adjusted net earnings of 33% compared with the same quarter from a year earlier. For the first nine months of 2025, there was an even more impressive leap in adjusted earnings to $410 million compared with $135 million for the first nine months of 2024. The biggest factor in this leap for the period was a roughly $260 million swing from a $227 million loss to positive net earnings of $32 million for its Westinghouse segment.

Stripping out the net effect of Westinghouse, which I doubt we will see repeated over and over again going forward, Cameco's earnings gains in the first nine months of 2025 were less impressive. Its mining segment saw earnings before tax rise by 11% over the previous year, and its fuel services segment gained a more impressive 82%. The net effect of its fuel services segment on the company is somewhat limited, given that most of the earnings came from the mining segment.

Much depends on uranium prices and other factors, but based on the latest financial results reported by the company, it would take many years to achieve an eightfold increase in earnings, which is what it would take for it to grow into its valuation. Cameco is therefore trading at valuation levels that are, in my view, too far ahead of market fundamentals.

Latest uranium market developments and prospects.

The uranium spot price bottomed in late 2016 and started a prolonged and at first timid recovery in 2017, just as I predicted it would in a late 2016 article. It has been on a sustained upward trajectory since then.

The demand growth factor is there to arguably sustain the continued recovery in uranium prices going forward, but it is by no means as impressive as the hype surrounding the conceptual thesis that nuclear reactors will power countless AI data centers.

As we can see, there are currently 440 reactors in operation, with a further 62 under construction around the world. Assuming that all the reactors under construction will become operational within a decade, with zero or very few old reactors taken offline, we are looking at a roughly 2%/year increase in the number of operating reactors.

In addition to these regular reactors, we can expect the spread of small modular reactors to pick up speed in the coming years. The scale of this trend is hard to predict, with many actors around the world in various stages of the commencement of large-scale deployment of these reactors. It could add up to a sizable increase in uranium demand growth within a few years.

With these demand dynamics in mind, Cameco sees a growing gap of uncovered demand from utilities, which in theory should lead to higher uranium prices.

As of 2024, the world produced 71,000 tonnes of uranium from mines, or about 157 million pounds, and it covered 90% of the World's demand according to the World Nuclear Association. Assuming that mined supplies more or less remain stuck at current levels, based on the forecast above, by 2040, mined supplies will only cover about 40% of the expected demand.

This looks like a bullish situation for uranium prices going forward. The problem is that it runs counter to my estimate of demand growth from new regular reactors coming online to be only about 2%/year. I have doubts that small modular reactors can boost demand enough to cover the difference. It might be worth exercising some caution in expecting the uranium market to continue the robust rally we saw so far this decade at the pace of price gains that we experienced. I am overall optimistic that uranium prices will continue to rise from here in the coming years; however, it might not be as robust as some investors might expect.

Investment implications.

In light of Cameco's valuation no longer being aligned with fundamentals, as I see it, I decided to exit my position in this stock. I intend to start building it up again if its share price declines to more reasonable levels. My current target is to start buying when its P/E ratio drops below 100, at which point I will continue to buy incrementally. The next incremental purchase after the innitial buy, will be when its P/E ratio approaches 80.

Though I sold my entire Cameco position, I still hold a position in the Global X Uranium ETF (URA), which continues to provide some exposure to the uranium mining and the nuclear power industry. I also continue to hold a minor position in UR-Energy (URG).

Cameco makes up nearly a quarter of the URA ETF. I therefore indirectly still have a sizable exposure to Cameco through this ETF.

The share price of UR-Energy is highly dependent on high-risk, high-potential-reward investing, since its financial results do not reflect its valuation in any way. It currently has a market cap of $720 million, while revenues are expected to come in at $27 million for 2025. It is losing money on those revenues. The sizable reserves it is sitting on, as well as expectations of potential production ramp-up supported by those reserves and associated infrastructure in place, make it an interesting investment opportunity, but not the type of investment that I want to build a sizable position in.

Cameco remains my number one choice in terms of building a sizable position in uranium mining and the broader nuclear power industry. It has a solid track record and a healthy financial position. Its costs for servicing debt in the first nine months of 2025 came in at $85.1 million, on revenues of nearly $2.3 billion, meaning that interest costs took up about 3.7% of revenues. I see a rate of 5% as being on the threshold of it being potentially dangerous, because at that point, interest costs are taking up far too much of a company's internal resources. I sold most of my uranium positions recently in response to the dramatic rise in the share price of my holdings. Cameco was my core position within the segment, and as I rebuild my uranium position, I intend to build it around this stock again.

Risks to my thesis.

There is always a chance that, despite the high valuations, there will be no sizable pullback in uranium mining stocks, including Cameco. If uranium prices keep going up steadily from here, chances are that uranium mining stocks will follow on the way up, despite being arguably heavily overvalued. In that event, the risk is that of an opportunity missed.

Conclusions.

Cameco was my stock pick of the decade, at the start of the decade, and by the end of this decade, I still expect it to be one of the best performers, measured from January 2020 until the end of this decade in December 2029. Given the stellar performance of its share price, it already has a good head start in that regard. It does not even have to perform spectacularly in the second half of the decade to still be a top contender as a top pick for the decade.

I expect to see Cameco's share price reach new all-time highs. The fundamentals dictate more limited upside from here relative to the performance we saw since the start of the decade. For this reason, the buy-and-hold strategy for this stock is no longer optimal. Buying the dips and selling the rallies, as it continues on the way up, is the way to play this going forward. At the moment, I have this stock as a hold, waiting for the next buying opportunity.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Fed Chair Pick--Today?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump administration prepares for Warsh Fed chair nomination, reports. (00:16) GrubHub going after rivals by removing fees on orders over $50. (01:02) Apple (AAPL) edges up after Q1 revenue jumps 16% on record iPhone sales. (02:07)

This is an abridged transcript.

The Trump administration is preparing for the president to name a new chair of the Federal Reserve.

Reports say that President Trump will nominate Kevin Warsh to succeed Jerome Powell.

President Trump told reporters on Thursday evening that he plans to announce his pick to lead the US central bank this morning.

It’s important to note that earlier in the day at a Cabinet meeting, the president said he would announce the pick next week.

Warsh, who has long been among the favorites for the Fed position, is considered a relatively hawkish choice. If nominated and confirmed, he would succeed Powell, whose term ends in May.

We’re taking a deeper look at this story in the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter. The link to sign up is in the show notes section.

GrubHub is removing service and delivery fees on restaurant orders over $50.

The new policy is already in place for some users but a broader launch is set for February 2.

With free delivery already offered to paid subscribers, GrubHub hopes that by extending free delivery to orders over $50, consumers will switch from DoorDash (DASH) and Uber Eats (UBER). Both companies charge a delivery fee of as much as $8 along with a 10% to 15% service fee. Also, Uber Eats (UBER) charges another $2 for orders below $10.

GrubHub is also hoping to avoid “checkout abandonment,” in which customers drop large orders with high delivery fees.

DoorDash (DASH) carries 71% of the market, 23% for Uber Eats (UBER), GrubHub only 4%.

The news briefly weighed on shares of Uber Technologies (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH), though both recovered modest losses and were higher at Thursday’s close.

Premarket DASH is down 1.2% and UBER is down 0.9%.

Apple (AAPL) released first quarter fiscal 2026 financial results that were driven by record-breaking iPhone sales.

For the quarter ended December 31, Apple reported earnings per share of $2.84 versus the consensus estimate of $2.67.

Revenue for the quarter increased 15.7% to total $143.76B, which was more than the $138.52B estimate. Product revenue increased 16% to $107.7B, which was led by iPhone revenue surging 23%.

Apple CEO Tim Cook said, "iPhone had its best-ever quarter driven by unprecedented demand, with all-time records across every geographic segment, and Services also achieved an all-time revenue record, up 14 percent from a year ago."

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Microsoft reportedly inks $750 million cloud deal with AI firm Perplexity

Trump files $10B lawsuit against IRS, Treasury Department over tax-return leak

Samsung SDI U.S. arm wins battery supply deal, seen linked to Tesla ESS, Yonhap reports

Catalyst watch:

  • Church & Dwight (CHD) will hold its Analyst Day event.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 1% at $64/barrel. Bitcoin is down 2.5% at $82,000. Gold is down 6% at $5,031.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.1% and the DAX is up 0.9%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Deckers Outdoor (DECK) +15% - Shares jumped after the company posted record FQ3 profit and revenue, driven by strong demand for UGG and HOKA across both wholesale and DTC channels.

data.description:
Goal and Strategy

Seeks to deliver high risk-adjusted returns through investment in investment-grade securities across multiple sectors.

Market Review

Bonds delivered fourth-quarter gain. U.S. investment-grade bonds logged their fourth straight quarterly gain. The volatile period witnessed the longest U.S. government shutdown in history, mounting job cuts, tepid consumer sentiment and growing division among Federal Reserve (Fed) policymakers.

Economy remained resilient. The government shutdown canceled or delayed key economic data, but available metrics indicated resilience. The U.S. economy gathered strength in the third quarter, logging an annualized growth rate of 4.3%, compared with 3.8% in the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter showed economic activity slowed but generally remained expansionary.

Fed easing continued. The Fed cut rates by 0.25% in October and again in December, dropping the target lending rate to a range of 3.5% to 3.75%. Labor market concerns prompted the easing, as the November unemployment rate reached 4.6%, the highest since September 2021. Meanwhile, growing division among Fed policymakers tempered expectations for future rate cuts.

Treasury yields were mixed. Treasury yields were volatile, and they ended the three-month period mixed. Labor market concerns and Fed easing contributed to a 0.14% decline in the two-year Treasury yield to 3.28%. Meanwhile, the yield on the 10-year Treasury rose slightly from 4.15% to 4.17%. The yield curve between two and 10 years steepened.

Inflation slowed. Inflation moderated in the quarter. However, due to the government shutdown, the Bureau of Economic Analysis delayed the core personal consumption expenditures price index, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge. Overall, inflation expectations moderated, and Treasury inflation-protected securities underperformed nominal Treasuries.

Sector performance was positive. All sectors of the broad investment-grade bond market advanced in the quarter, led by the outperforming mortgage-backed securities (MBS) sector. Treasuries outperformed investment-grade corporates but lagged the broad bond market. High-yield corporates outpaced the broad investment-grade market and investment-grade corporates.

Portfolio Performance Review

Sector allocations contributed. Our sector weightings drove the portfolio’s performance, largely due to an underweight position relative to the benchmark in Treasuries and an overweight in securitized securities. An out-of-index position in high-yield credit also aided results.

Security selection boosted results. Security selection aided performance, largely in the investment-grade credit and agency MBS sectors. Our selections among government and emerging markets securities also modestly contributed. These results more than offset negative selection effects among asset-backed securities, where maturity extension risk among a nonportfolio holding dragged down the entire subsector.

Yield curve effects slightly contributed. Overall, duration and yield curve positioning modestly aided overall performance. This was largely due to curve positioning in the U.S. and duration effects from an out-of-index position in the U.K.

Portfolio Positioning

Economic growth should improve. We believe the U.S. economy may gain ground in the first half of 2026, as fiscal stimulus, including approximately $150 billion in consumer tax benefits, unfolds. We believe a more pro-growth regulatory backdrop and continued business spending on artificial intelligence should also aid the economic backdrop.

Fed policy unclear. We expect inflation may inch higher in the first half of 2026 before stabilizing midyear. Modest growth, persistent above-target inflation and lingering labor market concerns may limit Fed action in 2026. If the Fed does ease, we expect only one rate cut. However, President Donald Trump soon will name a new Fed board chair, which could alter the monetary policy outlook.

Securitized sector still offers value. We continue to favor select securitized and high-yield credits, which we believe offer attractive value and yields versus other securities. With spreads tight, we’re reluctant to broadly add investment-grade credit and will continue to emphasize security selection. We favor duration exposure in the U.K., where we see more rate cut potential.

The letter ratings indicate the credit worthiness of the underlying bonds in the portfolio and generally range from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Generally, as interest rates rise, the value of the securities held in the fund will decline. The opposite is true when interest rates decline.

Source: Bloomberg Index Services Ltd. Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index represents securities that are taxable, registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission, and U.S. dollar-denominated. The index covers the U.S. investment-grade fixed-rate bond market, with index components for government and corporate securities, mortgage pass-through securities, and asset-backed securities.

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities (Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities (ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points (BPS): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for “carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index (CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration (CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or “cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest- paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures (usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets (EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve (Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts (Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation (GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield (HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade (IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard& Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A “market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher- yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into "pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing (QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool. ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard “swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns (or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct "full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills (maturing in one year or less), notes (maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds (maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes (VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic (daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any "inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.
