data.title: Bitdeer: Markets Are Pricing A Miner, But Full Stack Compute Ahead
data.description:
Last August, I argued that Bitdeer’s (BTDR) vertical integration thesis, which I have long held on to, was finally being validated. For most of the past two fiscal years, the market seemed skeptical that a mining firm could successfully design its own chips while also scaling infrastructure and executing a pivot to AI compute, thus BTDR traded at compressed valuation for most of 2023 and 2024 compared to peers because the R&D spend to build the proprietary hardware was not yet validated. Fast forward to today, the record revenues we are seeing shows that Bitdeer is delivering on its in-house chip strategy.

Bitdeer has officially grown from a single-focus Bitcoin (BTC-USD) miner to a diversified compute powerhouse. With the launch of its in-house chip and mining machine roadmap first laid out in 2024, the fourth release of the chip, SEAL04 chips, have passed its latest verification tests where they were reported to have achieved between 6 - 7 J/Th efficiency, with mass production targeted for Q1 2026 (as reported in Bitdeer's December 2025 production and operations update). And the 570 MW AI-ready pipeline in Ohio slated to be energized in the second half of this year (out of a 3 GW global pipeline spread across the U.S., Norway, and Malaysia), are the near term catalysts to watch.

While the broader market remains fixated on Bitcoin miners’ HPC pivots and lease deals at the moment, they could be missing the infrastructure arbitrage Bitdeer has. Bitdeer is currently sitting on a power portfolio twice the size of some HPC/AI peers in addition to in-house chip design, yet BTDR trades like a capital constrained pure play miner.

It seems the market assumes Bitdeer is behind on AI because they haven't announced a multi-year hyperscaler lease deal yet. What the market might be missing is that Bitdeer is building vertical ownership and an “as-a-Service” AI business model here. Bitdeer is designing both the silicon (now at SEAL04 chips) and will be managing the infrastructure, and selling compute capacity directly to its clients, with potential for higher margins. Though it might look unattractive from the start because headline multi billion HPC deals have been the market’s obsession lately.

We can attest to the tangible execution gains from the deployment of the SEAL chips and SEALMINER machines so far, which began as internal prototypes about two years ago. By using the SEALMINER A1 and A2 for self mining, Bitdeer avoided the high markups typically paid to hardware vendors like Bitmain. This has allowed the company to efficiently scale hashrate.

The crux of it is that Bitdeer’s hashrate has surpassed that of a giant like Mara Holding (RIOT) lately, and Bitdeer now controls about 71 EH/s of total hashrate under management and about 55 EH/s of self mining hashrate. The direct driver of this level of scaling has been the vertical integration strategy, which has been my core thesis since I initiated coverage in June 2024 at around $7 stock price. There has been large scale deployment of SEALMINER A2 machines at the company.

Thanks to those deployments, Bitdeer's Q3 2025 revenue was a record for the company at $169.7 million, and a 173.6% YoY increase. Remember that Q2 2025 revenue was also a record for the company at the time it was released. While total revenue only increased sequentially by ~9%, gross profit soared 218% sequentially from $12.8M to $40.8M, while adjusted EBITDA increased by 148% QoQ to reach $43 million. The main takeaway here is that Bitdeer beat Q2’s record top-line numbers sequentially by 9.1% but saw better operating leverage in profitability lines

GAAP net loss was $266.7 million, of which $247.6 million of that loss came from the fair value change of derivative liabilities related to convertible senior notes.

Bitdeer currently diversifies its revenue stream from four different sources, of which the self-mining segment remains the highest contributor to the top line to date. In Q3, self-mining revenue surged 120.7% sequentially. Self-mining increased from 38% of total revenue in Q2 to 77% of total revenue in Q3.

The self-mining revenue surge, matches the 112% jump in self-mining capacity from 16.5 EH/s in Q2 to 35.0 EH/s at the end of Q3 (remember I mentioned that has since increased to 55.2 EH/s post Q3).

Also note how revenue from SEALMINER sales dropped by 83% QoQ in Q3. This is where the whole vertical integration strategy starts to make sense. By being a hardware manufacturer, Bitdeer at its discretion, can dynamically route sales of the proprietary built SEALMINERs or prioritize deployment for its own self-mining hashrate for a particular period, as mining economics dictates.

This is what Bitdeer did in Q3 (as the revenue composition shows); they prioritized in-house deployments of the SEALMINERS (reflected in the jump in exahash). The sequential results confirm that this pivot (prioritizing in-house self mining) was the right call in Q3. Despite the top-line only growing by single digits, the bottom-line metrics (gross profit and adjusted EBITDA) improved greatly because Bitdeer captured the entire value chain (you can verify the figures in the first table shared earlier in this piece). Basically, as Bitdeer deployed their own chips, their cost to generate each dollar of revenue dropped. This is typically unheard of for a miner scaling at this speed (doubling hashrate in a single quarter). Typically for miners, more hashrate means a linear increase in costs which temporarily compresses margins.

Understanding Bitdeer's AI Pivot

While a portion of the market might be awaiting a lease deal similar to that the likes of IREN (IREN), TeraWulf (WULF), among others, have seen lately, I’d say Bitdeer's AI pivot is a bit more ambitious. It is now clear that Bitdeer plans to operate a full-stack cloud model; the recent NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 clusters deployment in Malaysia earlier this month points towards that strategy. Bitdeer didn’t announce a leasing partner for the Malaysia site, but rather deployed the NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 infrastructure directly.

​Bitdeer is an Nvidia (NVDA) cloud partner, and being an Nvidia cloud partner means Bitdeer has been vetted to deliver full-stack NVIDIA infrastructure on the hardware, software, and architectural layers. Bitdeer achieved the status of Preferred Cloud Service Provider [CSP] in the NVIDIA Partner Network in November 2023. This partnership is the backbone of the $2 billion ARR management targets.

What I believe the market might be missing in this AI pivot is that Bitdeer's effective vertical integration through SEAL chips and miners gives them lots of potential for cash flow from mining to fund these AI purchases without needing to lean on a tech giant for a multi-billion dollar lease deal to validate their strategy.

As the results in Q3 and subsequent operational updates have shown, the expansion in gross margins is on a scale much higher than revenue growth, and between the aggressive ramp-up in hashrate and the potential to achieve some of the lowest efficiencies (J/Th) when SEAL04 chips launch soon, the favorable economics of seeing the top-line accelerate while gross margin accelerate even higher is bound to continue.

Potential Near-Term Catalysts For BTDR

When the Q4 report drops in February, it will likely be the definitive proof of the vertical integration strategy's success. At ~55.2 EH/s currently, and based on the October to December 2025 monthly operations updates, in which 511 BTC was self-mined in October, 526 BTC in November, and 636 BTC in December, revenue from self-mining alone is expected to be ~$160 million (at ~$95,000 average BTC spot price in Q4).

Given the higher hashrate and BTC production jump in Q4, I believe Q4 is on track to be Bitdeer's third consecutive quarter of record revenue and sequential revenue growth.

I also anticipate management to provide guidance for full year 2026 for active AI cloud revenue potential. In both the last September and October production updates, management reiterated the aim at a $2 billion plus ARR, but if management formalizes the path to that $2 billion ARR target via the Ohio site with clearer near term milestones that the market can understand and will see as feasible, I believe the Bitcoin mining discount currently depressing the stock will begin to evaporate.

If it Goes According to Plan, How Will BTDR be Valued?

I believe if the 200 MW target for AI workload is reached later this year and the path to the $2 billion ARR becomes more visible, BTDR could command a premium compared to peers who have signed lease deals only in their HPC/AI pivot. Valuation could rerate toward premium, more like pure play AI cloud companies like CoreWeave (CRWV) or Nebius (NBIS).

CoreWeave currently trades around 9.3x sales. If we apply even a conservative 5x-6x multiple to Bitdeer’s projected $2 billion ARR, the AI business alone would be worth $10 billion – $12 billion, more than 3x the company's current total market cap of ~$3.3 billion. Though I expect BTDR to always have a slight discount compared to CRWV or NBIS, because the market will always likely factor in risks from the Bitcoin mining segment in the company's sum-of-the-parts valuation.

Risks and Takeaway

The biggest risk to this vertical integration and AI pivot theses is execution speed. Bitdeer already faces a class action lawsuit from a faction of investors regarding SEALMINERS A4 and SEAL04 chip delays, thus management must deliver the Ohio site on schedule. Any delay in the Q3 2026 power-up target would give the bears a reason to keep the miner discount firmly in place.

BTDR isn't being priced yet like an AI focused cloud provider with potential for high margin recurring ARR. The AI pivot differs from most Bitcoin mining peers. Bitdeer targets a full stack cloud model where it will own virtually everything, including the customer relationships.

Back to back record revenue and the rise in hashrate to become a top miner in terms of mining capacity, surpassing a giant like RIOT proves the vertical integration thesis is still intact.

data.title: U.S. Fed Keeps Interest Rates On Hold As Economic Outlook Improves
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Reserve decided to keep its key overnight interest rate on hold at its first policy meeting of 2026. Sam Chai, Vice President, Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management, breaks down the Fed’s decision and explains why he expects policymakers to start cutting again in the second half of the year.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie - The US Federal Reserve has put a pause on interest rates, keeping them at a range between 3.5% and 3.75%. Joining us for more is Sam Chai of TD Asset Management. And Sam, when you look at today's announcements, did anything really jump out at you?

Sam Chai - So I would say that today's decision to -- for the Fed to hold is a pretty widely expected decision, for a few good reasons. For one, the Fed has already delivered three consecutive cuts over the past meetings. Policy rate now is currently at 3.75%. And here I quote Chair Powell is now "within the range" of estimates of where the neutral rate is. So there's no argument that it's now much less restrictive, so a good place for the Fed to wait and see a bit.

On the labor market front, we have been seeing some a bit more stabilization in data of late. So December job growth was around 50,000, which is a solid data point. Unemployment rate actually went down slightly as well, so it's a good sign. And these are acknowledged in the latest statement as well.

Finally, on the inflation front, continued to be steady, I would say. Core CPI is around 2.5% to 3% range, so still above the Fed's 2% target but nothing too concerning for the Fed as well. So overall, there is no change to forward guidance from the Fed. And expectation is that we could continue to see a holding pattern in the near months.

There are two dovish dissents today from both Governor Waller and Miran, not unexpected by the market. There is this tiny dovish cohort, but more importantly, the bulk of the committee is still with the Chair Powell and prefer to be in a holding pattern right now.

Anthony Okolie - So given the fact that the economy is somewhat resilient right now, what's the likelihood that we could see a rate cut later this year?

Sam Chai - So I think the starting place would be, first, for us to revisit where the Fed's forecast of long-term neutral rate to be. And that is currently around 3%, meaning that there is still some room for policy rate to go lower before the Fed starts to be concerned about refueling upside inflation risk by the monetary policies. And so with that in mind, I see there are two paths for us to see more rate cuts this year. The first path is a bad news rate cuts. By that, I mean if labor market suddenly deteriorates further in the coming months, we could see rate cuts as early as next meeting or even the meeting after the next, as we know the Fed wants to continue to secure the soft landing for the US.

On the other hand, there could also be good news cut. The Fed continued to expect that inflation will slightly pick up in the coming months. But around end of first half, beginning of second half, we would expect the bulk of the tariff pass-through inflation effect to already materialize. So if inflation at that time is still relatively reasonable, then it opens up room for the Fed to cut further at that time, and on good inflation news. So considering both possibilities, I do see there's a decent chance we see several cuts by the Fed by the end of this year.

Anthony Okolie - All right. So, given that, let's talk about bond yields and what they're telling us about Fed policy, where Fed policy could be headed, especially given the uncertainty around a new leadership for the Fed as well as the pending Supreme Court decision on tariffs. What are bond yields telling us right now?

Sam Chai - So the US rates market currently priced in the first rate cut around June to July and two full cuts by the end of this year. And it's no coincidence that the market has priced in the first rate cut by June, because that is also when we expect, to your point, the new Fed chair to come to take the reins for the FOMC meetings. And market does expect that the new Fed chair will be shifting towards a more dovish reaction function, as well.

On the second point on Supreme Court rulings, so the Supreme Court ruling is expected to be against the Trump administration's on tariffs. Polymarket currently assigned 70% odds for that. But the market is not naive, right? We know the White House is likely going to find alternative channels to implement their tariff policy. So in the near term, inflation risks remain to the upside. Hence why the market thinks that it's likely the Fed will be mindful of this and be in a holding pattern for the first half and then start to cut in the second half of this year.

Anthony Okolie - I want to switch to fiscal policy because we've got the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which is making its way through the system this year. How might that influence the Fed's thinking on rate cuts down the road?

Sam Chai - So based on the range of estimates for the effect of the One Big Beautiful Bill, expectations for a roughly, call it, 0.3% to 0.5% contribution to US GDP growth this year.

Anthony Okolie - So there could be a bit of a tailwind there?

Sam Chai - That is correct, from consumer stimulus and CapEx stimulus. In particular, on the consumer stimulus front, it amounts to over $100 billion. And it's expected to be pretty front-loaded and acutely felt in the first half of this year, which is another reason why the market -- this is another reason for why the market expects that we can be in a holding pattern because of the support for the economy in the first half. And we see perhaps more easing in the second half of this year.

Anthony Okolie - Okay. Let's talk about the US dollar, which has had a pretty tough year. It's still under pressure against the basket of currencies. Where do you see the dollar going from here?

Sam Chai - So very interesting question, maybe a bit of context in the US dollar trend, as well. So in recent months, US dollar has actually been in a wide consolidation range after falling materially in the first half of last year. But in the past two weeks, there has been a lot of volatility, to your point, and downside pressure, as well.

For one, rising geopolitical and policy rates from the US side has refueled this de-dollarization trend. For one, there's also concerns about a potential US-Japan joint intervention, as well. We also have a rising US government shutdown risk in the moment, too. And yesterday, President Trump also commented that he doesn't worry too much about a weak dollar. But then, just today, Treasury Secretary Bessent mentioned that the US still prefers a strong-dollar policy, so a lot of dollar volatility there, as you can imagine.

Overall, I would say that we are sympathetic to this bearish dollar theme. And over the medium term, we do see that the risk is skewed to the downside for US dollar on the expectation that we could see further US dollar hedging flows as well as possibly a dovish shift in Fed reaction function in later this year.

Anthony Okolie - Now, as you mentioned, there's a lot of noise out there. What are the key indicators that you'll be keeping an eye on in the next weeks and months ahead as the Fed considers its next move?

Sam Chai - I think top of mind would be just the nomination of the new Fed chair, who will be selected, and also, if there's any new messages from the Fed chair because the market is keenly monitoring any shift in Fed reaction functions going forward. On the economic data front, on the inflation side, expectation is for a gradual rise in inflation, possibly from more tariff pass-throughs. So very important, in my opinion, to monitor the core goods component, which is most sensitive to tariff-induced inflation.

So if we are seeing a meaningful reacceleration of core goods prices, that would be a major unwelcome surprise for both the market as well as the Federal Reserve. And they will really restrain the Fed's ability to dial down policy rates further.

On the labor market front, we continue to be in this low-firing, low-hiring regime. So very important to monitor data, including initial jobless claims, layoff data, because if we start to see a consistent pickup in these, these will be meaningful red flags and, again, a key risk for the market because both the Fed and the market continue to expect a benign macro backdrop right now and is not pricing in that scenario.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
